title: Imeglimin
subtitle: First-in-Class Mitochondrial Antidiabetic — Mechanism, TIMES Program & Sequencing, Verified, With a Skepticism Section
authors: User-provided clinical/trial summary, fact-checked against the TIMES 1-3 trial publications and the Hallakou-Bozec mechanism paper
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat); cross-checked against TIMES 1 (Diabetes Care 2021), TIMES 2 (Diabetes Obes Metab 2022), TIMES 3 (Diabetes Obes Metab 2022), and Hallakou-Bozec et al. (Diabetes Obes Metab 2021)

items.title: Why does imeglimin's mitochondrial mechanism produce a dual effect on both insulin secretion and insulin action?
items.content:
Imeglimin partially inhibits Complex I and corrects deficient Complex III activity in the mitochondrial respiratory chain, reducing reactive oxygen species formation and preventing mitochondrial permeability transition pore opening.
This mitochondrial correction has two downstream effects: in pancreatic beta-cells, it increases cellular NAD+ generation via the nicotinamide salvage pathway (through NAMPT), amplifying calcium mobilization and glucose-stimulated insulin secretion (GSIS); in liver and skeletal muscle, the corrected mitochondrial function is linked to reduced hepatic glucose output and improved insulin signaling.
keyPoints: Partial Complex I inhibition + corrected Complex III activity → reduced ROS, prevented mPTP opening.
Beta-cells: increased NAD+ via NAMPT/salvage pathway → amplified Ca++ mobilization → enhanced GSIS.
Liver/muscle: linked to reduced hepatic glucose output and improved insulin signaling.
items.reference: Hallakou-Bozec S et al., "Mechanism of action of Imeglimin: A novel therapeutic agent for type 2 diabetes," Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2021;23(3):664-673 (see Skepticism section for the source's journal citation error).

items.title: What is imeglimin structurally, and how does it relate to metformin?
items.content: Imeglimin is a tetrahydrotriazine derivative, structurally distinct from biguanides such as metformin despite some superficial resemblance. It targets mitochondrial bioenergetics directly rather than acting primarily through AMPK activation, the dominant mechanism attributed to metformin.
keyPoints: Imeglimin: tetrahydrotriazine derivative, a distinct chemical class from biguanides.
Targets mitochondrial bioenergetics directly, a different primary mechanism than metformin.
items.reference: Imeglimin structural/mechanistic classification, Hallakou-Bozec et al., Diabetes Obes Metab, 2021.

items.title: What is the standard dosing regimen for imeglimin?
items.content: Imeglimin is given orally at 1,000 mg twice daily — the dose used across all three pivotal TIMES trials (TIMES 1, 2, and 3) and the approved dosing for Twymeeg in Japan.
keyPoints: 1,000 mg orally, twice daily (BID).
Same dose used across TIMES 1, 2, and 3.
items.reference: TIMES 1 trial dosing protocol (Dubourg J et al., Diabetes Care, 2021).

items.title: Why is imeglimin specifically positioned for patients with mitochondrial dysfunction and beta-cell exhaustion as the primary pathology?
items.content:
Because imeglimin's mechanism directly targets mitochondrial bioenergetics in both beta-cells and peripheral tissue, it addresses the "twin defects" of type 2 diabetes — insulin resistance and failing beta-cell insulin secretion — simultaneously.
This makes it conceptually well-suited to phenotypes where early beta-cell failure is a dominant driver, such as is commonly described in lean, non-obese Asian patients with type 2 diabetes, rather than phenotypes driven predominantly by obesity-related insulin resistance.
keyPoints: Targets both insulin resistance and beta-cell failure ("twin defects").
Conceptually suited to phenotypes with early beta-cell failure (e.g., lean/non-obese Asian T2DM).
items.reference: Yabe D et al., "Imeglimin: A novel oral antidiabetic agent targeting mitochondrial function," Journal of Diabetes Investigation, 2022 — contextualizing imeglimin for Asian T2DM pathophysiology.

items.title: How does renal function actually affect imeglimin dosing, and is "strict contraindication" below eGFR 15 the accurate framing?
items.content:
Imeglimin is excreted largely unchanged by the kidneys, so renal impairment raises systemic exposure. Population pharmacokinetic modeling supports a reduced dose of 500 mg twice daily for patients with eGFR 15-45 mL/min/1.73 m², and a longer dosing interval (rather than an outright contraindication) for eGFR below 15.
The practical takeaway is dose-and-interval adjustment scaled to renal function, not a simple binary "contraindicated below 15" rule — see the Skepticism section for more on this nuance.
keyPoints: eGFR 15-45: reduced dose (500 mg BID) supported by PK modeling.
eGFR <15: a longer dosing interval is the modeled approach, not a flat contraindication.
Renal dosing is a graded adjustment, not a simple on/off rule.
items.reference: Imeglimin population pharmacokinetics and dose-adjustment modeling for renal impairment (PubMed 36847203; researchgate.net/publication/357373804).

items.title: Why does imeglimin carry a low risk of hepatotoxicity and drug-drug interactions compared with many older oral antidiabetics?
items.content: Imeglimin undergoes virtually no hepatic metabolism via CYP450 enzymes and is eliminated largely unchanged renally, which limits both its potential to cause hepatocellular injury and its potential to interact with other CYP450-metabolized drugs.
keyPoints: Minimal CYP450-mediated hepatic metabolism.
Low potential for hepatotoxicity.
Low potential for CYP450-mediated drug-drug interactions.
items.reference: Chevalier B et al., "Pharmacokinetics and Safety of Imeglimin in Subjects with Hepatic Impairment," Clin Pharmacokinet, 2020; Reilhac C et al., "Assessment of the Potential for Drug-Drug Interactions of Imeglimin," Clin Drug Investig, 2020.

items.title: What is the main dose-limiting tolerability concern with imeglimin, and how does it compare with older biguanides?
items.content: Imeglimin's main tolerability issue is dose-dependent gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarrhea), which can worsen with systemic accumulation in renal impairment. It does not carry the lactic acidosis boxed-warning history associated with early biguanides, reflecting its distinct mitochondrial target and elimination pathway.
keyPoints: Main tolerability issue: dose-dependent GI upset.
No lactic acidosis boxed-warning history, unlike early biguanides.
items.reference: Imeglimin tolerability data across TIMES 1-3 (Dubourg J et al., Diabetes Care 2021; Diabetes Obes Metab 2022).

items.title: What did the TIMES 1 monotherapy trial demonstrate, and how large was the effect?
items.content: TIMES 1 was a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled phase 3 trial in 213 Japanese patients (106 imeglimin, 107 placebo). At 24 weeks, imeglimin 1,000 mg twice daily produced a placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction of -0.87% (95% CI -1.04 to -0.69; p<0.0001), with a similar overall safety profile to placebo.
keyPoints: 213 patients (106 imeglimin, 107 placebo).
Placebo-adjusted HbA1c change at 24 weeks: -0.87% (p<0.0001).
Safety profile similar to placebo.
items.reference: Dubourg J et al., "Efficacy and Safety of Imeglimin Monotherapy Versus Placebo... (TIMES 1)," Diabetes Care, 2021;44(4):952-959.

items.title: What did the TIMES 2 trial actually evaluate, and what was its headline result?
items.content:
TIMES 2 was a 52-week, open-label, multicenter phase 3 trial in 714 Japanese patients, evaluating imeglimin 1,000 mg twice daily either as monotherapy or added on to a wide range of existing background agents (DPP-4 inhibitor, thiazolidinedione, alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, glinide, biguanide, SGLT2 inhibitor, sulfonylurea, or injectable GLP-1 receptor agonist).
The overall, whole-trial result reported in available summaries is an HbA1c decrease from baseline of -0.92% (LS mean) at 52 weeks. This is a long-term safety/efficacy trial across a broad real-world background population, not a head-to-head comparison isolating each background drug class — see the Skepticism section regarding the specific per-class breakdown some sources cite.
keyPoints: 714 patients, 52-week open-label trial, monotherapy or add-on to 8 different background agent types.
Overall HbA1c change from baseline: -0.92% (LS mean) at 52 weeks.
Design is a broad long-term safety/efficacy study, not a controlled head-to-head matrix of each background class.
items.reference: Dubourg J et al., "Long-term safety and efficacy of imeglimin as monotherapy or in combination with existing antidiabetic agents... (TIMES 2)," Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2022; Poxel/Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma topline results announcement, 2019.

items.title: What did the TIMES 3 insulin add-on trial demonstrate?
items.content: TIMES 3 randomized 215 Japanese patients (108 imeglimin, 107 placebo) inadequately controlled on insulin to add-on imeglimin 1,000 mg twice daily or placebo for 16 weeks, followed by a 36-week open-label extension. The placebo-adjusted HbA1c change at week 16 was -0.60% (95% CI -0.80 to -0.40; p<0.0001), and the improvement versus baseline was sustained through 52 weeks at -0.64%.
keyPoints: 215 patients on background insulin, randomized to imeglimin or placebo.
Placebo-adjusted HbA1c change at 16 weeks: -0.60% (p<0.0001).
Effect sustained through 52 weeks (-0.64% vs baseline) in the open-label extension.
items.reference: Reilhac C / Dubourg J et al., "Efficacy and safety of imeglimin add-on to insulin monotherapy... (TIMES 3)," Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2022.

items.title: Why might an academic framing of imeglimin's monotherapy effect understate its clinical role?
items.content: Viewed purely through TIMES 1's monotherapy result (-0.87% HbA1c), imeglimin's glucose-lowering looks comparable to a DPP-4 inhibitor or a low-dose SGLT2 inhibitor used alone. Positioning it as a combination/add-on agent for patients with a mitochondrial-dysfunction or beta-cell-exhaustion phenotype, rather than as a monotherapy competing head-to-head with other classes, better reflects how it has actually been studied and marketed (e.g., across the diverse add-on backgrounds tested in TIMES 2 and TIMES 3).
keyPoints: TIMES 1 monotherapy effect size is comparable to other oral agents used alone.
Imeglimin has been extensively studied and positioned as an add-on/combination agent.
Clinical framing as a phenotype-targeted combination agent differs from a pure monotherapy comparison.
items.reference: TIMES 1 (Diabetes Care 2021) vs. TIMES 2/3 (Diabetes Obes Metab 2022) trial designs and positioning.

items.title: Why does the skeptic's case against imeglimin center on the absence of cardiovascular outcome data?
items.content: SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists have large, dedicated, multi-year cardiovascular outcome trials (CVOTs) demonstrating reductions in heart failure admissions, stroke, and/or mortality. Imeglimin's pivotal program (TIMES 1-3) was designed to demonstrate glycemic efficacy and safety in Japanese patients, not to test hard cardiovascular endpoints — so, as of the available evidence, there is no dedicated CVOT establishing that imeglimin reduces cardiovascular events or mortality.
keyPoints: SGLT2i/GLP-1RA have dedicated CVOTs showing hard-outcome benefit.
TIMES 1-3 were glycemic efficacy/safety trials, not CVOTs.
No dedicated cardiovascular outcomes trial for imeglimin exists in the available pivotal program.
items.reference: TIMES 1-3 trial designs (Diabetes Care 2021; Diabetes Obes Metab 2022) — none designed as a CVOT.

items.title: Who economically benefits from imeglimin, and why is it priced at a premium?
items.content: Imeglimin is a patented, first-in-class molecule co-developed by Poxel (originator) and commercialized in Japan by Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma (now Sumitomo Pharma) under the brand name Twymeeg, approved in Japan in June 2021 — the first country to approve it. As a novel patented molecule with no generic competition, it commands premium pricing, with the cost typically borne by the healthcare system and patient for what is most often used as a secondary or tertiary add-on agent.
keyPoints: Originator: Poxel SA. Japan commercialization partner: Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma (Twymeeg).
First approved in Japan, June 2021.
Premium, patented pricing with no generic competition.
items.reference: Poxel/Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma TWYMEEG approval announcement, June 2021 (sumitomo-pharma.com/news/20210623.html).

items.title: How does imeglimin's early trajectory echo the historical launch pattern of DPP-4 inhibitors?
items.content: Like the DPP-4 inhibitors when first launched (circa 2006), imeglimin was introduced with a novel mechanism, favorable tolerability, weight neutrality, and minimal hypoglycemia risk, with its pivotal program concentrated in Japan and other Asian markets before any Western approval — paralleling how DPP-4 inhibitors were also disproportionately emphasized in Asian populations, where beta-cell-dominant T2DM phenotypes are more prevalent. This is a plausible historical analogy rather than a proven, formally established repeating pattern.
keyPoints: Novel mechanism, weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia risk at launch — like early DPP-4 inhibitors.
Pivotal program concentrated in Japan/Asia first.
An interpretive historical analogy, not a formally proven recurring law.
items.reference: TIMES program being conducted entirely in Japan (Diabetes Care 2021; Diabetes Obes Metab 2022); general historical context of DPP-4 inhibitor launches.

items.title: Why is the Hallakou-Bozec mechanism paper's journal citation in the source incorrect?
items.content:
The source cites "Hallakou-Bozec S, et al. (Endocrinology, 2021)" for imeglimin's mechanism-of-action paper.
This paper, "Mechanism of action of Imeglimin: A novel therapeutic agent for type 2 diabetes," was actually published in Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism (2021;23(3):664-673), not the journal Endocrinology. The mechanistic content the source describes (Complex I/III effects, NAD+ salvage pathway, GSIS amplification) is accurately drawn from this paper — only the journal name is wrong.
keyPoints: Source's journal ("Endocrinology") is wrong.
Correct journal: Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2021.
The mechanistic content itself is accurately described; only the citation is wrong.
items.reference: Hallakou-Bozec S et al., Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2021;23(3):664-673.

items.title: Why is the source's specific TIMES 2 per-class HbA1c breakdown the single most important claim to verify independently?
items.content:
The source presents a precise four-way breakdown of TIMES 2 results by background drug class — add-on to DPP-4i: -0.92%, biguanide: -0.67%, SGLT2i: -0.57%, GLP-1 RA: -0.12% — and builds its entire "expert practitioner" DPP-4i-synergy argument on this table.
Publicly available summaries of TIMES 2 instead report -0.92% as the trial's overall, whole-population HbA1c change from baseline at 52 weeks (714 patients pooled across monotherapy and all background-agent groups combined) — not specifically the DPP-4i subgroup. The other three subgroup-specific deltas could not be independently confirmed from publicly available sources. This does not mean the source's numbers are wrong, but the specific per-class breakdown — the central numerical claim of this entire topic — needs to be checked directly against the original paper's subgroup tables before being treated as established.
keyPoints: Source's central claim: a 4-way per-class breakdown (DPP-4i -0.92%, biguanide -0.67%, SGLT2i -0.57%, GLP-1RA -0.12%).
Verified instead: -0.92% is reported as the OVERALL 52-week TIMES 2 result, not specifically the DPP-4i subgroup.
The other three class-specific deltas are unconfirmed from available sources — check the original paper's subgroup data directly.
items.reference: TIMES 2 trial, Dubourg J et al., Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2022; topline results announcement (sumitomo-pharma.com/news/20191220.html) — neither confirms a per-background-class breakdown matching the source's table.

items.title: Why is framing eGFR <15 as "strictly contraindicated" an overstatement of the actual renal dosing evidence?
items.content:
The source states imeglimin is "strictly contraindicated" below eGFR 15 mL/min/1.73 m².
Population pharmacokinetic modeling instead supports a longer dosing interval (a graded adjustment) rather than an absolute contraindication at this level, alongside a reduced 500 mg twice-daily dose for the 15-45 mL/min/1.73 m² range. "Use with extreme caution and a modified regimen" is more accurate than "strictly contraindicated," though clinical experience and outcome data in this severe-impairment group remain limited.
keyPoints: Source's framing ("strictly contraindicated" below eGFR 15) overstates the modeling data.
PK modeling instead supports a longer dosing interval at eGFR <15, not an outright ban.
Reduced dose (500 mg BID) is the modeled approach for eGFR 15-45.
items.reference: Imeglimin population pharmacokinetics and dose-adjustment modeling for renal impairment (PubMed 36847203).

items.question: Through what mitochondrial mechanism does imeglimin enhance glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in pancreatic beta-cells?
options: By directly blocking ATP-sensitive potassium channels, like a sulfonylurea
By increasing cellular NAD+ via the nicotinamide salvage pathway (NAMPT), amplifying calcium mobilization in response to glucose
By stimulating GLP-1 receptor signaling directly
By inhibiting SGLT2 in the pancreas
items.explanation: Imeglimin increases cellular NAD+ generation via the NAMPT-mediated nicotinamide salvage pathway, which enhances glucose-stimulated insulin secretion through amplified calcium mobilization — a mechanism distinct from sulfonylureas, GLP-1 agonists, or SGLT2 inhibitors.
items.reference: Hallakou-Bozec S et al., Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2021.

items.question: What is the standard oral dosing regimen for imeglimin used across the TIMES 1-3 pivotal trials?
options: 500 mg once daily
1,000 mg twice daily
2,000 mg once daily at bedtime
250 mg three times daily
items.explanation: Imeglimin 1,000 mg taken orally twice daily was the dose used consistently across TIMES 1, TIMES 2, and TIMES 3.
items.reference: TIMES 1 dosing protocol, Dubourg J et al., Diabetes Care, 2021.

items.question: A patient with an eGFR of 30 mL/min/1.73 m² is being considered for imeglimin. Based on population pharmacokinetic modeling, what is the most appropriate approach?
options: Imeglimin is strictly contraindicated at this eGFR and must not be used
Use the standard 1,000 mg twice-daily dose with no adjustment
Use a reduced dose of 500 mg twice daily, consistent with PK modeling for eGFR 15-45 mL/min/1.73 m²
Switch immediately to a sulfonylurea instead
items.explanation: Population PK modeling supports a reduced dose of 500 mg twice daily for patients with eGFR 15-45 mL/min/1.73 m², rather than an outright contraindication at this level.
items.reference: Imeglimin renal-impairment dose-adjustment modeling (PubMed 36847203).

items.question: Why does imeglimin carry a low risk of clinically significant drug-drug interactions?
options: It is highly protein-bound and displaces other drugs from albumin
It undergoes virtually no hepatic metabolism via CYP450 enzymes
It is a strong CYP3A4 inducer that clears other drugs quickly
It cannot be co-administered with any other medication
items.explanation: Imeglimin undergoes virtually no CYP450-mediated hepatic metabolism, which substantially limits its potential for clinically significant drug-drug interactions.
items.reference: Reilhac C et al., Clin Drug Investig, 2020.

items.question: What was the placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction at 24 weeks in the TIMES 1 monotherapy trial?
options: No significant difference from placebo
items.explanation: TIMES 1 demonstrated a placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction of -0.87% at 24 weeks (p<0.0001) with imeglimin 1,000 mg twice daily monotherapy.
items.reference: Dubourg J et al., Diabetes Care, 2021;44(4):952-959.

items.question: What was the placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction at 16 weeks when imeglimin was added to background insulin therapy in TIMES 3?
options: No measurable effect
items.explanation: TIMES 3 found a placebo-adjusted HbA1c reduction of -0.60% at week 16 when imeglimin was added to insulin therapy, sustained at -0.64% versus baseline through the 52-week open-label extension.
items.reference: TIMES 3 trial, Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2022.

items.question: What is the central evidentiary gap underlying the skeptic's case against imeglimin, relative to SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists?
options: Imeglimin has never been tested in a randomized controlled trial
Imeglimin's pivotal program (TIMES 1-3) has no dedicated cardiovascular outcomes trial demonstrating reduced hard cardiovascular events or mortality
Imeglimin causes more hypoglycemia than sulfonylureas
Imeglimin has not been approved in any country
items.explanation: Unlike SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists, which have dedicated multi-year cardiovascular outcome trials, imeglimin's pivotal TIMES 1-3 program was designed around glycemic efficacy and safety endpoints, leaving a gap in hard cardiovascular outcome evidence.
items.reference: TIMES 1-3 trial designs (Diabetes Care 2021; Diabetes Obes Metab 2022).

items.question: Who commercializes imeglimin (Twymeeg) in Japan, and who is the original developer?
options: Developed and commercialized entirely by Pfizer
Originated by Poxel SA, commercialized in Japan by Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma (now Sumitomo Pharma)
Developed by Eli Lilly, commercialized by Novo Nordisk
Originated and commercialized solely by a Japanese generic manufacturer
items.explanation: Imeglimin was originated by Poxel SA (France) and commercialized in Japan under the brand Twymeeg through a partnership with Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma (now Sumitomo Pharma), approved in Japan in June 2021.
items.reference: Poxel/Sumitomo Dainippon Pharma TWYMEEG approval announcement, June 2021.

items.question: A study guide presents a precise four-way TIMES 2 breakdown attributing a -0.92% HbA1c change specifically to the "add-on to DPP-4 inhibitor" subgroup. What does independent verification of publicly available TIMES 2 summaries show?
options: This exact subgroup breakdown is confirmed verbatim in all available secondary sources

Available summaries report -0.92% as the OVERALL 52-week result across the whole 714-patient trial population (monotherapy plus all background agents combined) — not specifically isolated to the DPP-4i subgroup
TIMES 2 never measured HbA1c at all
TIMES 2 only tested imeglimin as monotherapy, with no background agents
items.explanation: Publicly available TIMES 2 summaries report -0.92% as the trial's overall HbA1c change from baseline across the full 714-patient population (monotherapy and all background-agent subgroups combined), not as a figure specifically isolated to the DPP-4 inhibitor add-on subgroup — the specific per-class breakdown requires direct verification against the original paper's subgroup tables.
items.reference: TIMES 2 trial, Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, 2022; topline announcement, sumitomo-pharma.com/news/20191220.html.

items.question: A source states imeglimin is "strictly contraindicated" at eGFR below 15 mL/min/1.73 m². What does the actual population pharmacokinetic modeling support instead?
options: The statement is fully accurate; no dose can be used below this threshold
A longer dosing interval (a graded adjustment) at eGFR <15, rather than an absolute contraindication, alongside a reduced 500 mg twice-daily dose for eGFR 15-45
No renal dose adjustment is needed at any eGFR level
Imeglimin should be doubled in severe renal impairment to maintain efficacy
items.explanation: Population pharmacokinetic modeling supports a graded approach — a reduced dose (500 mg BID) for eGFR 15-45 mL/min/1.73 m² and a longer dosing interval for eGFR below 15 — rather than a flat "strictly contraindicated" rule.
items.reference: Imeglimin renal-impairment dose-adjustment modeling (PubMed 36847203).
